takeaway: Computer hardware is the physical machine. It 'includes the physical parts of a computer, such as the central processing unit (CPU), random-access memory (RAM), motherboard, computer data storage, graphics card, sound card, and computer case.'
Computers represent everything in base-2: 'the binary system is used by almost all modern computers and computer-based devices' because two symbols (0 and 1) map cleanly to two electrical states.
The smallest unit is a bit (a single 0 or 1); 'the byte is a unit of digital information that most commonly consists of eight bits,' enough to represent 256 distinct values.
The CPU is the brain that runs programs: its 'electronic circuitry executes instructions of a computer program, such as arithmetic, logic, controlling, and input/output (I/O) operations.'
The CPU works by repeating one loop — fetch, decode, execute — that it 'follows from boot-up until the computer has shut down in order to process instructions.'

A running program is just machine code in memory that the processor steps through: 'the CPU continues running its fetch-execute cycle as usual,' using an instruction pointer to track its place in RAM.

RAM is fast, temporary working memory that loses its contents at power-off; 'a random-access memory device allows data items to be read or written in almost the same amount of time irrespective of the physical location.'
The industry splits the two: 'the faster and volatile storage components are referred to as memory, while slower persistent components are referred to as storage' — RAM versus disk.

The operating system is the manager that sits between apps and hardware: it is 'system software that manages computer hardware and software resources, and provides common services for computer programs.'
The only language a CPU truly understands is machine code: it 'is classified as native with respect to its host CPU since it is the language that the CPU interprets directly.'

Humans write readable source code that must be turned into machine code: 'execution of a program requires an implementation,' done by either a compiler (translate ahead of time) or an interpreter (translate as it runs).

The internet runs on TCP/IP, a layered set of protocols (link, internet, transport, application) whose guiding rule is to 'be conservative in its sending behavior, and liberal in its receiving behavior.'

Every device on a network has an IP address — a numerical label for routing; as the classic framing goes, 'a name indicates what we seek. An address indicates where it is. A route indicates how to get there.'
DNS is the internet's phone book: it 'translates readily memorized domain names to the numerical IP addresses needed for locating and identifying computer services.'
TCP makes delivery trustworthy: it provides 'reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of octets (bytes) between applications running on hosts communicating via an IP network.'
The web speaks HTTP: 'HTTP is a request–response protocol in the client–server model,' where the browser asks (GET, POST) and the server answers.

MDN's full flow: 'the browser sends an HTTP request message to the server, asking it to send a copy of the website to the client. This message... is sent across your internet connection using TCP/IP,' arriving as packets the browser reassembles.
The web is built on the client-server model: 'clients... initiate communication sessions with servers, which await incoming requests' and return resources.
A server is just another computer playing a role: 'a server is a computer or software system that provides data, resources, or services to other computers called clients on a computer network.'

The cloud is renting someone else's servers on demand: 'a paradigm for enabling network access to a scalable and elastic pool of shareable physical or virtual resources with self-service provisioning and administration on demand.'
HTTPS is HTTP with a lock on it: 'HTTPS signals the browser to use an added encryption layer of SSL/TLS to protect the traffic' from interception and tampering.
TLS is the cryptography behind the padlock: a protocol that secures connections via a handshake, and 'its use in securing HTTPS remains the most publicly visible.'
GeeksForGeeks overview of common internet protocols including TCP/IP, SMTP, FTP, SFTP, HTTP, HTTPS, POP3, and Telnet, explaining the purpose and key characteristics of each.

ByteByteGo deep dive on database selection criteria covering scalability, performance, consistency trade-offs across relational, NoSQL, NewSQL, and time-series databases to guide engineering decisions.
Comprehensive 2011 primer on how browsers work internally — covering rendering engines, networking, JavaScript interpreters, the DOM, CSS parsing, and the full pipeline from URL to painted page.
18-minute Simplilearn video explaining encryption fundamentals — what it is, how it works, and its role in cryptography and network security.
Hands-on tutorial building a Docker-like container from scratch using Linux tools unshare, mount, and pivot_root, explaining how mount namespaces provide filesystem isolation for containers.
